network.overall_strategy: The network models prebiotic alanine synthesis as a highly connected bipartite graph grounded in the literature, with racemic alanine generated robustly by multiple hydrothermal and surface routes and L-alanine emerging only in a later biochemical selection/amplification context. The main convergent hubs are HCN, acetaldehyde, pyruvate, and alpha-aminopropionitrile. Hydrothermal chemistry contributes CO2 reduction, pyruvate formation, Fischer-Tropsch-type precursors, and direct pyruvate reductive amination; surface chemistry contributes Strecker, cyanosulfidic, HCN oligomer, spark-generated precursor supply, and Bucherer-Bergs chemistry; biochemical/prebiotic stages model conversion of racemic alanine into an L-enriched pool via RNA-mediated or network-level chiral selection consistent with the literature that all direct abiotic routes are racemic. Ten distinct pathways to the target are encoded as alternative valid sequences through shared hubs.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, anoxic, H2-rich, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 to one-carbon reduced carbon on Fe-S catalytic sites.
reactions.reasoning: Fe-S hydrothermal catalysis of CO2 reduction to small organics is central to iron-sulfur world and alkaline vent models, with greigite and mackinawite as plausible catalysts and vent conditions matching serpentinization-driven H2 supply (Wachtershauser, 1988; Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2

conditions.other: anoxic vent setting; Fe/Ni mineral mediated carbon fixation

reactions.mechanism: Multicarbon CO2 fixation on Fe-bearing mineral surfaces yields alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented prebiotic branch-point intermediate and its formation from CO2 under Fe/Ni-promoted conditions is supported by hydrothermal and reverse-TCA analog chemistry (Muchowska, Varma, and Moran, 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal formaldehyde production from CO2 and H2
reactions.mechanism: Fe(Ni)S-catalyzed reduction of CO2 by H2 generates reduced C1 compounds including formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 in hydrothermal vent analogs has been demonstrated and provides a key feedstock connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to surface aldehyde chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Spark discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced atmosphere

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration/adsorption surfaces; electrical discharge is primary driver
conditions.other: spark discharge in atmospheric apparatus followed by aqueous trapping

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge generates nitriles and aldehydes from reduced gases; products dissolve into aqueous phase for downstream Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Miller-Urey experiments showed alanine arises through in situ formation of HCN and acetaldehyde, which then undergo Strecker chemistry in the aqueous phase; mineral surfaces here are included as plausible concentrative agents in surface pools, though discharge is the primary energy source (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of acetaldehyde from HCN feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system with mineral sulfide support
conditions.other: UV irradiation, neutral pH, cyanosulfidic conditions

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reductive homologation of HCN in the presence of sulfide generates aldehyde precursors including acetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland established photoredox homologation routes from HCN to simple sugars and aldehydes, and Patel et al. integrated HCN/H2S/UV systems chemistry at 35C. Mineral sulfide and TiO2 surfaces are included as plausible natural photochemical supports in subaerial settings (Ritson and Sutherland, 2012; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.other: mild aqueous pH; acetaldehyde first forms imine then cyanide addition

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to give alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the canonical Strecker route to alanine and the most extensively studied prebiotic pathway; Miller explicitly identified Strecker chemistry as the mechanism in spark experiments, and isotopic work supports aminonitrile intermediacy (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide
reactions.mechanism: Partial nitrile hydrolysis converts alpha-aminopropionitrile to the corresponding amide.
reactions.reasoning: Sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile via alaninamide is the accepted Strecker progression to alanine (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of alaninamide to racemic alanine
reactions.mechanism: Amide hydrolysis yields free alpha-amino acid as racemic alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Completion of the Strecker pathway from alaninamide to alanine is well established and produces DL-alanine rather than a single enantiomer (Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with phosphate-rich neutral solution
conditions.other: UV irradiation, neutral pH; reported ~70% aminonitrile from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Phosphoro-cyanosulfidic activation channels acetaldehyde, cyanide, and reduced sulfur into efficient aminonitrile formation.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported alpha-aminopropionitrile formation at about 70% yield from acetaldehyde under neutral cyanosulfidic conditions, making this a major high-flux route into the alanine network (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN

conditions.other: requires concentrated HCN, >~0.01 M; eutectic freezing or evaporation can concentrate feedstock

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes through dimeric and trimeric nitriles to tetrameric diaminomaleonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is a central hub process linking amino acid and nucleobase chemistry, documented in classic Oró and Ferris studies under concentrated cyanide conditions (Oro, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic conversion of HCN oligomers to racemic alanine

conditions.other: alkaline oligomerization followed by hydrolytic workup

reactions.mechanism: Polymeric HCN material and DAMN-derived products hydrolyze to amino acids including alanine, while parallel DAMN photochemistry feeds adenine chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomers and black polymers gives amino acids including alanine, and DAMN is also the gateway to adenine-related chemistry, making this a documented convergence/branch point (Oro, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs conversion of pyruvate to 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs type chemistry; decarboxylation-derived CO2 capture into hydantoin cycle

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate-derived aminonitrile captures CO2 to cyclize into 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. identified a prebiotically relevant Bucherer-Bergs route in which pyruvate, cyanide, ammonia, and captured CO2 form 5-methylhydantoin as a key intermediate bridging amino acid and nucleobase chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine
reactions.mechanism: Ring opening hydrolysis of the hydantoin yields carbamoylated amino acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs sequence explicitly proceeds through 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine under warm aqueous conditions (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine
reactions.reasoning: Final hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine is part of the prebiotic Bucherer-Bergs pathway reported for pyruvate-derived alanine synthesis (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Pyrite-driven reductive amination of pyruvate to racemic alanine

conditions.other: coupled to oxidative pyrite formation; anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds to pyruvate-derived imine/iminium equivalent and mineral-mediated reduction gives alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Direct reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to pyrite formation was demonstrated by Hafenbradl and coworkers and is a major hydrothermal route consistent with iron-sulfur world chemistry (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wachtershauser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron oxyhydroxide reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: mixed Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral phases, optimal 1:1 ratio

reactions.mechanism: Surface-bound alpha-keto acid undergoes reductive amination under mixed-valence iron redox cycling.
reactions.reasoning: Barge et al. showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline mixed-valence iron conditions with maximum yield at Fe(II):Fe(III)=1:1, consistent with vent-proximal sediments; magnetite/Fe-bearing mineral phases are appropriate hydrothermal analogs (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Ni/H2-catalyzed reductive amination of pyruvate to racemic alanine
reactions.mechanism: Catalytic hydrogenation of the pyruvate-ammonia adduct yields alanine under mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. demonstrated nickel-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine at 22C, pH 7-8, and 5 bar H2; hydrothermal mineral context here reflects vent-available Fe minerals interfacing with Ni catalysis rather than replacing the reported Ni catalyst (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of racemic alanine
reactions.mechanism: Sulfide-mineral mediated carbonyl/cyanide/thioester-type chemistry generates amino acids including alanine under hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Huber and Wachtershauser reported hydrothermal amino acid synthesis including alanine from CO, KCN, and CH3SH on metal sulfide catalysts at 80-120C, establishing a direct vent route independent of classical Strecker hydrolysis (Huber and Wachtershauser, 2006).

reactions.name: Biochemical/prebiotic chiral selection from racemic alanine to L-alanine

conditions.other: mineral interfaces, protocell compartments, geothermal cycling, possible RNA-mediated stereoselection after prior symmetry breaking

reactions.mechanism: Not a direct abiotic enantioselective synthesis; rather, L-alanine is selected from a racemic pool through network-level amplification or stereoselective incorporation in an emerging biochemical context.
reactions.reasoning: The literature strongly indicates that all direct prebiotic alanine syntheses are racemic, while the most plausible route to biological L-alanine is indirect via terrestrial symmetry breaking and later biochemical selection, potentially mediated by homochiral RNA or autocatalytic network effects rather than a single alanine-forming reaction (Glavin et al., 2025; Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Ozturk et al., 2025; Blackmond et al., 2025).

pathways.name: Classic spark-Strecker alanine pathway
pathways.description: Reduced atmospheric discharge generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which undergo Strecker synthesis and sequential hydrolysis to racemic alanine, followed by biochemical selection to L-alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic high-yield aminonitrile pathway
pathways.description: HCN-based photoredox chemistry produces acetaldehyde and then alpha-aminopropionitrile under phosphoro-Strecker conditions before hydrolysis to alanine and later L-selection.

pathways.name: Direct surface Strecker from exogenous acetaldehyde
pathways.description: When acetaldehyde and HCN are already available in surface pools, direct Strecker synthesis yields alpha-aminopropionitrile and then alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and polymeric nitriles, whose hydrolysis releases alanine, then enters L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to alanine via pyrite redox
pathways.description: CO2 fixation produces pyruvate, which is reductively aminated on FeS/H2S-pyrite systems to racemic alanine prior to biochemical L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate to alanine via mixed-valence iron
pathways.description: Pyruvate generated hydrothermally undergoes reductive amination in alkaline Fe(II)/Fe(III) mineral settings, then feeds L-selection.

pathways.name: Ni/H2 mild reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermally available pyruvate is hydrogenated with ammonia under Ni/H2 conditions at room temperature to alanine, then selected to L-form.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway from pyruvate
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia, cyanide, and CO2 to form 5-methylhydantoin, then N-carbamoyl alanine, then alanine before L-selection.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino acid synthesis
pathways.description: CO, KCN, and methanethiol yield alanine directly on hydrothermal metal sulfide catalysts, followed by L-selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction furnishes pyruvate; surface Bucherer-Bergs chemistry converts it through hydantoin intermediates to alanine, which enters biochemical chiral selection.
